module.label: Pediatric DKA, HHS, or hyperglycemic crisis

use_when: Child or adolescent with known diabetes, new diabetes symptoms, hyperglycemia, ketones, vomiting or abdominal pain with diabetes context, missed insulin, pump failure, euglycemic DKA risk, or possible pediatric HHS.

A pediatric hyperglycemic-crisis workup needs current weight, hydration/perfusion and neurologic monitoring, glucose/ketone/acid-base thresholds, potassium-safe fluids, insulin timing without bolus, cerebral edema escalation, precipitant review, and diabetes-team handoff.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult DKA/HHS pathway.
Child has immediate airway, shock, coma, seizure, or suspected cerebral edema requiring resuscitation; activate local emergency/PICU/retrieval pathway while documenting focused findings.
Stable mild hyperglycemia without ketones/acidosis or dehydration; use pediatric diabetes or local outpatient/inpatient hyperglycemia guidance.
Known specialty endocrine/PICU protocol where a local pediatric DKA/HHS protocol overrides this general clinician-support module.

modifier_add_on_policy.rationale: This validated pediatric DKA/HHS pack includes source-localizing precipitant and infection questions; generic infection prompts should not replace DKA-specific source localization or cerebral-edema monitoring.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric DKA: hyperglycemia or diabetes context plus acidosis and moderate or large ketones; euglycemic DKA remains possible with SGLT2 exposure, fasting, pregnancy, or poor intake
differentialBuckets.action: Confirm glucose, blood beta-hydroxybutyrate or urine ketones, venous pH/bicarbonate, electrolytes, potassium, sodium trend, urea/creatinine, and clinical dehydration before classifying severity or treatment safety.
differentialBuckets.rationale: CHQ defines pediatric DKA using glucose above 11 mmol/L, venous pH below 7.3 and/or bicarbonate below 18 mmol/L, and moderate/large ketones; treatment depends on hydration, potassium, insulin timing, and neurologic risk.

source.implementation_notes: Do not silently reuse adult ADA/HHS thresholds for pediatric routing.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis, mixed DKA/HHS, or euglycemic DKA.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Positive criteria route to pediatric DKA protocol, weight-based fluids, delayed insulin start, potassium-safe fluids, cerebral edema monitoring, and diabetes/PICU escalation as indicated.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this diagnostic frame; pediatric DKA is defined by biochemical criteria plus clinical severity and source-backed management thresholds.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A normal or only mildly elevated glucose does not exclude euglycemic DKA when acidosis and ketones are present.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric HHS: marked hyperglycemia with hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, and no major acidosis; mixed DKA/HHS can occur
differentialBuckets.action: Calculate effective osmolality and compare glucose, pH/bicarbonate, ketones, sodium, and fluid deficit before choosing insulin or fluid pace.
differentialBuckets.rationale: CHQ defines pediatric HHS as glucose above 33.3 mmol/L, effective osmolality above 320 mOsm/kg, ketones below 1.1 mmol/L, and pH above 7.25 and/or bicarbonate above 15 mmol/L.

source.implementation_notes: HHS always needs urgent specialist advice because fluid deficit and osmolality management differ from DKA.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state or mixed DKA/HHS.
differentialBuckets.management_change: HHS concern changes fluid replacement strategy, osmolality monitoring, insulin timing/dose, ICU/specialist involvement, and neurologic-risk monitoring.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this diagnostic frame; HHS is defined by source thresholds and specialist management rules.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not assume all high glucose is DKA; HHS may need more aggressive fluid replacement and slower insulin only after glucose stops falling with fluids.

differentialBuckets.label: Precipitating cause: new diabetes, insulin omission, pump/CGM failure, infection, dehydration, medication/SGLT2 exposure, pregnancy, psychosocial barrier, or recurrent DKA pattern
differentialBuckets.action: Ask source-localizing precipitant questions and order tests only when symptoms, fever, device issues, recurrence, or medication/pregnancy context make them management-changing.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH, NICE, ISPAD, and CHQ emphasize identifying the precipitating cause and recurrence context without using nonspecific leukocytosis alone as infection proof.

source.source_section: risk factors, recurrent DKA, and psychosocial support
source.implementation_notes: Recurrent DKA needs psychosocial and access review, not only acute protocol text.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: DKA/HHS precipitant, recurrence driver, or barrier to safe discharge.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Positive precipitant changes cultures/imaging, antibiotics, device removal, medication review, diabetes-team handoff, safeguarding/mental-health input, sick-day planning, and follow-up intensity.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this broad precipitant frame; management changes by source-specific history, exam, and test findings.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not order broad infection tests solely because DKA can cause leukocytosis; localize infection by symptoms, fever, exam, and senior judgment.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes precipitant workup toward source-localizing infection assessment while hypothermia or temperature instability can signal shock or severe illness.
safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes cultures/labs/imaging only when paired with source symptoms, exam findings, sepsis concern, or senior advice.
safetyChecks.options: ___ C/F / febrile / afebrile / hypothermic / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Antipyretics and timing can mask fever trajectory.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

source.implementation_notes: Keep temperature in baseline data, not physical exam.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate and blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and blood pressure with pediatric age-band interpretation.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia, hypotension, hypertension with bradycardia, or shock changes fluid urgency, neurologic concern, and ICU/retrieval escalation.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal rate or BP changes resuscitation, fluid bolus decision, cerebral edema concern, monitoring interval, and transfer threshold.
safetyChecks.options: HR ___ bpm / BP ___/___ mmHg / age-appropriate / tachycardic / bradycardic / hypotensive / hypertensive / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain, fever, anxiety, dehydration, cuff size, and cerebral edema affect interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to vital signs as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: assessment, dehydration, and cerebral oedema signs
source.implementation_notes: Bradycardia with hypertension is a cerebral edema/oedema warning sign.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate and oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, and current oxygen delivery.
safetyChecks.rationale: Kussmaul respirations suggest acidosis; hypoxia, exhaustion, pneumonia, or respiratory compromise changes resuscitation and transfer.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory data changes airway/breathing support, blood gas urgency, infection-source workup, monitoring level, and PICU/retrieval discussion.
safetyChecks.options: RR ___/min / SpO2 ___% room air or oxygen / age-appropriate / tachypnea / Kussmaul pattern / hypoxemia / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, pain, acidosis, bronchodilators, and shock affect respiratory rate.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate or oxygen saturation as baseline clinical data.

source.implementation_notes: Respiratory rate is baseline data; Kussmaul breathing is inspected as a separate maneuver.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status with AVPU or modified GCS
safetyChecks.action: Document AVPU or pediatric/modified GCS at baseline and repeat at protocol intervals; increase frequency for severe DKA or age under 2 years.
safetyChecks.rationale: NICE recommends neurologic monitoring and every-30-minute modified GCS/heart-rate checks for children under 2 years or severe DKA; cerebral edema often occurs early.
safetyChecks.management_change: Falling score, headache, vomiting, bradycardia, hypertension, cranial nerve signs, seizure, or abnormal posturing changes immediate senior/PICU management.
safetyChecks.options: Alert / responds to voice / responds to pain / unresponsive / GCS ___ / baseline differs / improving / worsening / not available
safetyChecks.findings_options: AVPU alert / AVPU voice / AVPU pain / AVPU unresponsive / modified GCS ___ / baseline differs / worsening / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Development, sedation, exhaustion, post-ictal state, disability baseline, and language barriers affect scoring.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to neurologic status as baseline safety data; trajectory directly changes management.

source.implementation_notes: Neurologic status is baseline safety data, not a generic physical exam bundle.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Document current measured or best estimated weight in kg before fluids, insulin, potassium, and emergency medicines.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric DKA fluids, insulin, potassium-containing fluids, mannitol or hypertonic saline, and transfer handoffs are weight-based.
safetyChecks.management_change: Missing weight blocks safe dosing, fluid deficit calculation, insulin rate, cerebral edema treatment, and medication safety checks.
safetyChecks.options: ___ kg measured / ___ kg estimated / local emergency estimate / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Use actual weight when possible; document when estimated during resuscitation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as baseline dosing data.

source.implementation_notes: Weight is baseline dosing data, not physical exam.

safetyChecks.label: Measure bedside glucose and point-of-care ketones
safetyChecks.action: Measure bedside blood glucose and blood beta-hydroxybutyrate when available; use urine ketones if blood ketones are unavailable.
safetyChecks.rationale: Glucose and ketones are core DKA/HHS classification data and guide dextrose addition, insulin safety, and resolution monitoring.
safetyChecks.management_change: High glucose with ketones/acidosis routes to DKA; very high glucose with low ketones and hyperosmolality routes to HHS; falling glucose triggers dextrose-containing fluids.
safetyChecks.options: BGL ___ mg/dL or mmol/L / ketones ___ mmol/L / urine ketones negative trace small moderate large / not available
safetyChecks.equipment_needed: glucometer and blood ketone meter or urine ketone strip
safetyChecks.limitations: Urine ketones lag behind blood beta-hydroxybutyrate and can misrepresent treatment response.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to bedside glucose/ketones; these are diagnostic and management-threshold data.

source.implementation_notes: Prefer blood ketones when available.

safetyChecks.label: Document intake, urine output, and fluid balance
safetyChecks.action: Document recent intake, urine output, emesis losses, IV/enteral fluids, and strict hourly fluid balance once treatment begins.
safetyChecks.rationale: DKA and HHS fluid plans depend on dehydration estimate, ongoing losses, anuria, potassium safety, and cerebral edema monitoring.
safetyChecks.management_change: Anuria blocks potassium in fluids; excessive losses or HHS changes fluid replacement; poor urine output changes renal, potassium, and transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.options: last void ___ hours ago / urine output ___ mL/kg/hr / intake ___ mL or poor intake / emesis losses ___ mL or episodes / oliguria / anuria / strict hourly fluid balance started / not available
safetyChecks.findings_options: last void ___ hours ago / urine output ___ mL/kg/hr / intake ___ mL or poor intake / emesis losses ___ mL or episodes / oliguria / anuria / strict hourly fluid balance started / not available
safetyChecks.equipment_needed: fluid balance chart and urine collection method
safetyChecks.limitations: Caregiver estimates can be imprecise; use measured output when possible.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to fluid balance as baseline treatment-safety data.

source.implementation_notes: Urine output is treatment-safety data, not a physical exam maneuver.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask age weight and diabetes context
requiredQuestions.text: What is the child's exact age, measured or estimated weight, known diabetes type if any, new diabetes symptoms, current diabetes team, and usual insulin or pump regimen?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask first for every pediatric hyperglycemic-crisis concern.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates new diabetes, known type 1 diabetes, type 2/HHS risk, device therapy, dosing safety, and adult-pathway exclusion.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: New diabetes, known T1D, type 2/HHS risk, device use, or missing weight changes diagnostics, fluid/insulin calculation, diabetes-team ownership, education, and follow-up.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: new diabetes symptoms / known type 1 diabetes / known type 2 diabetes / pump or CGM / long-acting insulin regimen / diabetes team known / weight confirmed / weight unknown / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; age, weight, diabetes type, and regimen are pediatric routing and dosing variables.

source.implementation_notes: Expose age/weight up front so adult DKA defaults cannot be applied silently.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask hyperglycemia ketone and acidosis symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Are polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, vomiting, abdominal pain, deep breathing, fruity breath, dehydration, confusion, or reduced alertness present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every suspected pediatric DKA/HHS or high-glucose/ketone presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies DKA symptom cluster, dehydration severity, respiratory compensation, cerebral edema risk, and HHS overlap.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive vomiting, abdominal pain, deep breathing, dehydration, or mental status change increases urgency for VBG, ketones, fluids, potassium safety, neurologic monitoring, and escalation.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: polyuria / polydipsia / weight loss / fatigue / vomiting / abdominal pain / deep or Kussmaul breathing / fruity breath / dehydration / confusion or drowsy / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; symptoms prompt source-defined biochemical confirmation and severity assessment.

source.implementation_notes: Keep as multi-select so endorsed and denied symptoms can be documented.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask insulin doses pump CGM and access barriers
requiredQuestions.text: Were basal or bolus insulin doses missed, delayed, vomited around, reduced, unavailable, or unaffordable, and is there pump/CGM failure, infusion-site problem, battery/supply issue, or sick-day plan breakdown?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for known diabetes, pump/CGM use, recurrent DKA, high ketones, or unexplained hyperglycemia.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies insulin omission, access issue, device failure, and preventable recurrence drivers.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive findings change pump discontinuation, insulin replacement, site inspection, supply/social-work plan, diabetes-team handoff, sick-day education, and safe discharge planning.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: missed basal / missed bolus / insulin unavailable / affordability or access barrier / pump failure / infusion site problem / CGM concern / no sick-day plan / doses taken as prescribed / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; insulin interruption and pump failure are management-changing precipitants.

source.source_section: risk factors, insulin omission, and recurrent DKA
source.implementation_notes: Do not frame omission as blame; document barriers and safety plan.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask vomiting intake and dehydration trajectory
requiredQuestions.text: How many times has the child vomited, can they keep fluids down, what was the last intake, how much urine output is present, and are thirst, dry mouth, dizziness, syncope, or worsening dehydration present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for vomiting, poor intake, dehydration, abdominal pain, or DKA/HHS concern.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Determines fluid deficit, shock risk, potassium safety, NPO status, and HHS fluid needs.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Poor intake, ongoing vomiting, oliguria/anuria, syncope, or severe dehydration changes fluids, potassium-in-fluid rules, antiemetic caution, monitoring, and disposition.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: ongoing vomiting / unable to keep fluids down / poor intake / dry mouth / marked thirst / dizziness or syncope / oliguria / anuria / urine output normal / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; hydration and urine output change treatment safety rather than diagnostic probability alone.

source.implementation_notes: Document urine output because potassium addition depends on renal output and serum potassium.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask source-localizing infection and precipitant symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Are fever, cough, work-of-breathing change, sore throat, urinary symptoms, flank pain, abdominal focality, diarrhea, skin wound, line or pump-site infection, sick contacts, recent steroids, or other stressor present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric DKA/HHS case because precipitant treatment prevents recurrence and missed infection.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes infection or physiologic stress without relying on nonspecific leukocytosis from DKA.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive source symptoms change targeted exam, cultures, urine testing, chest imaging, antibiotics, sepsis pathway, medication review, and admission handoff.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: fever / cough or respiratory symptoms / sore throat / dysuria or frequency / flank pain / focal abdominal pain / diarrhea / skin wound / pump or line site infection / sick contacts / recent steroids or stressor / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; source-localizing features drive targeted tests and treatment.

source.implementation_notes: CHQ notes high WCC is common in DKA and should not alone trigger sepsis labeling.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask cerebral edema and neurologic warning symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Are headache, recurrent vomiting, irritability, altered or fluctuating conscious state, slowed heart rate concern, high blood pressure concern, seizure, abnormal behavior, weakness, cranial nerve symptoms, or abnormal posturing present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask at baseline and with any deterioration during pediatric DKA/HHS treatment.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies cerebral edema/oedema warning signs and severe neurologic complications.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change immediate senior/PICU escalation, head-up positioning, hyperosmolar therapy consideration, fluid reassessment, and transfer; do not delay for imaging.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: headache / recurrent vomiting / irritability / altered conscious state / fluctuating conscious state / bradycardia concern / hypertension concern / seizure / abnormal behavior / weakness / cranial nerve symptom / abnormal posturing / none / unable to assess
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; cerebral edema signs are source-defined emergency triggers.

source.implementation_notes: Use both edema and oedema terms in UI text so clinicians recognize local spellings.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask HHS dehydration and osmolality features
requiredQuestions.text: Is there extreme thirst, very high glucose, prolonged polyuria, severe dehydration, confusion, obtundation, seizure, obesity or type 2 diabetes context, or low/absent ketone report despite marked hyperglycemia?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when glucose is very high, ketones are low, type 2 diabetes/obesity context exists, or mental status is abnormal.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pediatric HHS or mixed DKA/HHS that requires specialist advice and different insulin/fluid handling.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change osmolality calculation, specialist/PICU involvement, fluid strategy, slower insulin dose, and neurologic monitoring.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: very high glucose / marked thirst / prolonged polyuria / severe dehydration / confusion or obtundation / seizure / type 2 diabetes or obesity context / low or absent ketones / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; HHS uses biochemical thresholds and specialist management rules.

source.implementation_notes: HHS should not be hidden under routine DKA insulin timing.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask recurrent DKA psychosocial and safety context
requiredQuestions.text: Has the child had recurrent DKA, recent admissions, insulin restriction, eating disorder concern, depression or self-harm concern, caregiver conflict, school/supply barrier, housing/food insecurity, or difficulty contacting the diabetes team?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for recurrent DKA, missed insulin, adolescent, unsafe discharge concern, or unclear precipitant.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies recurrence drivers and unsafe discharge conditions that acute biochemical correction will not solve.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes diabetes educator/social work/mental-health/safeguarding involvement, sick-day plan, supplies, family teaching, and follow-up intensity.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: recurrent DKA / recent admission / insulin restriction / eating disorder concern / depression or self-harm concern / caregiver conflict / supply or school barrier / housing or food insecurity / diabetes team hard to reach / none endorsed / unable to obtain confidentially
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to psychosocial context; positive items change safe-discharge planning.

source.implementation_notes: Keep confidential adolescent context PHI-safe in copy/export.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask euglycemic DKA and adolescent context
conditionalQuestions.text: For adolescent, pregnancy possibility, fasting, eating disorder, low-carbohydrate intake, alcohol/substance use, SGLT2 inhibitor exposure, or recent bariatric/weight-loss medication context, are ketones or acidosis present despite near-normal glucose?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: pregnancy possible / fasting / eating disorder concern / low-carbohydrate intake / alcohol or substance use / SGLT2 inhibitor / weight-loss medication context / ketones with near-normal glucose / none / unable to obtain confidentially
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when euglycemic DKA risk, adolescent, pregnancy, fasting, SGLT2, or atypical glucose/ketone pattern is mentioned.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Prevents false reassurance from glucose below classic DKA thresholds when ketones and acidosis are present.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes ketone/VBG testing, pregnancy testing, medication review, diabetes/endocrine consultation, and DKA treatment despite lower glucose.

source.source_section: euglycaemic DKA note and adolescent considerations
source.implementation_notes: Do not let adult/pregnancy modifiers create authoritative unsupported recommendations outside pediatric scope.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; euglycemic DKA is a threshold/risk-context rule.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask hypernatremia anuria and potassium safety context
conditionalQuestions.text: Are hypernatremia, hyperosmolality, anuria, potassium above normal, potassium below normal, renal impairment, or ECG change reported before choosing potassium-containing fluids or insulin timing?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: hypernatremia / hyperosmolality / anuria / potassium high / potassium low / renal impairment / ECG change / potassium unknown / none / unable to obtain
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when sodium/osmolality/potassium/renal-output concern is mentioned or labs are available.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies children who need urgent specialist advice and prevents unsafe potassium or insulin sequencing.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive findings change potassium addition, insulin timing, ECG monitoring, senior/PICU advice, and fluid strategy.

source.implementation_notes: Potassium miscalculation is a known serious safety risk; make the exception logic visible.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to electrolyte-safety context; values directly change treatment.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask recurrent DKA insulin omission confidentially
conditionalQuestions.text: When recurrent DKA or insulin omission is possible, can the child/adolescent and caregiver be asked separately about access, distress, body image, self-harm, neglect, coercion, bullying, or unsafe home/school support?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: child/adolescent private history obtained / caregiver private history obtained / access barrier / diabetes distress / body image or insulin restriction / self-harm concern / neglect or coercion concern / school support barrier / safe supports present / unable to obtain confidentially
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when recurrent DKA, missed insulin, adolescent, or unsafe discharge context is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies high-risk recurrence and safety factors that can be missed if the caregiver-only story is accepted.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive findings change social work, psychology/psychiatry, safeguarding, diabetes educator, supply support, and discharge readiness.

source.source_section: recurrent DKA prevention and psychosocial intervention
source.implementation_notes: Use local privacy and safeguarding policy; keep export concise and PHI-safe.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to confidentiality/safeguarding context; positive findings change disposition and follow-up.

requiredExam.label: Test central capillary refill
requiredExam.technique: Press central skin such as sternum or forehead for 5 seconds, release, and time color return while considering ambient temperature and perfusion.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal / delayed / cool peripheries / mottled / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every suspected pediatric DKA/HHS when safe.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Dehydration severity, poor perfusion, or shock.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for DKA dehydration; perfusion findings change fluid urgency by guideline rule.
requiredExam.management_change: Delayed refill or poor perfusion changes fluid bolus decision, monitoring, senior/PICU escalation, and shock evaluation.
requiredExam.limitations: Cool environment, skin tone, fever, shock, and examiner technique affect interpretation.

source.implementation_notes: Individual perfusion maneuver; vital signs stay in safety data.

requiredExam.label: Inspect mucous membranes
requiredExam.technique: Inspect oral mucosa and tongue moisture without delaying resuscitation.
requiredExam.findings_options: moist / dry / very dry / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every suspected pediatric DKA/HHS when safe.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Dehydration severity and oral intake tolerance.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for mucous-membrane dryness in pediatric DKA; use it with guideline hydration, perfusion, weight, and urine-output data.
requiredExam.management_change: Dry mucosa supports dehydration assessment, fluid plan, NPO/PO status, and monitoring intensity.
requiredExam.limitations: Mouth breathing, fever, oxygen therapy, and recent fluids can affect findings.

source.implementation_notes: Keep as a single bedside maneuver rather than a bundled hydration exam.

requiredExam.label: Test skin turgor
requiredExam.technique: Gently pinch abdominal or thigh skin and observe recoil, interpreting with age, body habitus, and hydration context.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal recoil / reduced turgor / unreliable / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in suspected DKA/HHS as one part of dehydration assessment.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for skin turgor in pediatric DKA; use it with guideline hydration, perfusion, weight, and urine-output data.
requiredExam.management_change: Reduced turgor supports dehydration assessment and fluid/monitoring decisions when interpreted with perfusion, mucosa, weight, and urine output.
requiredExam.limitations: Young infants, malnutrition, obesity, and chronic skin changes reduce reliability.

source.implementation_notes: Avoid overestimating dehydration; CHQ notes deficit is often overestimated in DKA.

requiredExam.label: Observe work of breathing and Kussmaul pattern
requiredExam.technique: Observe chest/abdominal movement for deep sighing respirations, increased work of breathing, exhaustion, apnea, or pneumonia clues.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal effort / Kussmaul deep breathing / increased work / exhaustion / apnea concern / focal pneumonia clue / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every suspected DKA/HHS or acidosis concern.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Metabolic acidosis compensation, respiratory compromise, pneumonia/infection source, or impending fatigue.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for Kussmaul breathing in pediatric DKA; deep breathing is a clinical acidosis clue requiring VBG confirmation.
requiredExam.management_change: Kussmaul or increased work changes VBG urgency, oxygen/airway support, infection-source assessment, and escalation threshold.
requiredExam.limitations: Anxiety, pain, asthma, pneumonia, and fatigue can mimic or obscure Kussmaul breathing.

source.implementation_notes: This is a respiratory bedside maneuver; respiratory rate is documented separately.

requiredExam.label: Inspect pupils and eye movements
requiredExam.technique: Inspect pupil size/reactivity and observe eye movements for cranial nerve palsy or abnormal gaze when developmentally possible.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal / unequal pupils / poor reaction / cranial nerve palsy concern / abnormal gaze / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform at baseline and urgently with headache, altered status, vomiting, seizure, hypertension/bradycardia, or cerebral edema concern.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Cerebral edema/oedema, raised intracranial pressure, herniation risk, or focal neurologic complication.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for pupil or eye-movement findings in pediatric DKA; cranial nerve or pupil abnormalities are source-defined emergency signs.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal findings change immediate senior/PICU escalation, hyperosmolar therapy consideration, imaging deferral until stabilized, and transfer.
requiredExam.limitations: Age, sedation, hypoglycemia, post-ictal state, and cooperation affect assessment.

source.implementation_notes: Expose neurologic signs as specific maneuvers and monitoring data, not broad neuro exam prose.

requiredExam.label: Palpate abdomen for focal tenderness
requiredExam.technique: Gently palpate the abdomen for focal tenderness, guarding, distension, or peritonism when the child is stable enough.
requiredExam.findings_options: nonfocal mild pain / focal tenderness / guarding / distension / peritonism / unable or unsafe to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when abdominal pain, vomiting, focal abdominal symptoms, or surgical mimic concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: DKA abdominal pain versus appendicitis, pancreatitis, obstruction, peritonitis, or infection source.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for abdominal focality in pediatric DKA; use focal or peritoneal findings to redirect by guideline/source rationale.
requiredExam.management_change: Focal tenderness or peritonism changes imaging/lab choice, surgical review, antibiotics, and DKA precipitant workup.
requiredExam.limitations: DKA can cause nonspecific abdominal pain; reassess after stabilization and acidosis improvement.

source.implementation_notes: Keep abdominal palpation specific; do not label as generic focused exam.

conditionalExam.label: Auscultate lungs for infection source
conditionalExam.technique: Auscultate anterior and posterior lung fields for focal crackles, wheeze, reduced air entry, or signs of pneumonia when respiratory symptoms, fever, hypoxemia, or sepsis concern is present.
conditionalExam.findings_options: clear / focal crackles / wheeze / reduced air entry / unable to assess
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when source-localizing infection symptoms, fever, hypoxemia, cough, or respiratory distress is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, bronchiolitis/asthma mimic, sepsis source, or respiratory comorbidity during DKA.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for lung-source findings in pediatric DKA; use guideline/source rationale for targeted imaging and antibiotics.
conditionalExam.management_change: Focal lung findings change CXR, cultures, antibiotics, oxygen/respiratory support, and admission/transfer planning.
conditionalExam.limitations: Early pneumonia can have subtle findings; interpret with vitals, oxygen saturation, and symptoms.

source.source_section: precipitating cause and infection testing on senior advice
source.implementation_notes: Use targeted infection workup rather than broad fever metadata.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect insulin pump infusion site
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect insulin pump infusion site, CGM site, IV/line sites, skin wounds, and pressure areas for erythema, drainage, abscess, cellulitis, or tenderness.
conditionalExam.findings_options: normal / pump-site inflammation / line-site inflammation / cellulitis / abscess or drainage / unable or not applicable
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when pump/CGM/line use, fever, skin symptoms, recurrent DKA, or unclear precipitant is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Device failure, infusion-site infection, cellulitis, abscess, or line infection precipitating DKA.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for pump-site inspection in pediatric DKA; visible infection or device failure changes source treatment and insulin delivery plan.
conditionalExam.management_change: Positive findings change pump removal/replacement, culture or drainage consideration, antibiotics, diabetes-device review, and discharge supply plan.
conditionalExam.limitations: Respect privacy and document if site inspection is deferred.

source.source_section: insulin pump therapy and precipitating factors
source.implementation_notes: Pump/site findings should be traceable because they materially change the workup.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate peripheral pulses
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate radial and pedal pulses for volume and symmetry while noting coolness or mottling separately from monitor-derived heart rate.
conditionalExam.findings_options: normal volume / weak pulses / bounding pulses / asymmetric / unable to assess
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when dehydration, HHS, shock, poor perfusion, hypotension, or cardiovascular compromise is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Shock, severe dehydration, poor peripheral perfusion, or vascular compromise.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for peripheral pulse volume in pediatric DKA; pulse volume is a perfusion safety finding.
conditionalExam.management_change: Weak pulses change fluid bolus urgency, senior/PICU escalation, monitoring, and transfer threshold.
conditionalExam.limitations: Cold extremities and examiner technique affect interpretation.

source.implementation_notes: This is a physical perfusion maneuver; heart rate remains baseline safety data.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect pupils for neurologic deterioration
conditionalExam.technique: Repeat pupil/eye-movement check and observe for abnormal posturing, new weakness, seizure activity, or worsening response while emergency treatment is being arranged.
conditionalExam.findings_options: stable / worsening conscious state / new pupil or gaze abnormality / seizure / abnormal posturing / unsafe to delay care
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform immediately when cerebral edema/oedema concern or neurologic deterioration is present, without delaying emergency management.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Cerebral edema/oedema, raised intracranial pressure, herniation risk, or seizure complication.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for neurologic deterioration in pediatric DKA; source-defined deterioration is an emergency management trigger.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal or worsening findings trigger senior/PICU advice, head-up positioning, hypertonic saline or mannitol per protocol, fluid reassessment, oxygen, and transfer.
conditionalExam.limitations: Do not delay treatment for imaging or complete examination if cerebral edema is suspected.

source.implementation_notes: Immediate management should not wait for neuroimaging.

redFlags.label: Cerebral edema or oedema signs
redFlags.action: Treat altered or fluctuating consciousness, headache, recurrent vomiting, bradycardia, hypertension, cranial nerve palsy, seizure, or abnormal posturing as cerebral edema/oedema concern until senior/PICU team advises otherwise.
redFlags.rationale: CHQ and RCH list these as warning signs and advise immediate senior escalation; management should not wait for imaging.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Cerebral edema/oedema, raised intracranial pressure, herniation risk, or neurologic injury during DKA/HHS.
redFlags.management_change: Changes immediate escalation, head-up positioning, oxygen, hypertonic saline or mannitol per protocol, fluid reassessment, ICU/retrieval, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available; this is an emergency red-flag rule based on source-defined signs.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of one sign does not exclude evolving cerebral edema; repeat neurologic monitoring is required.

source.source_section: cerebral oedema signs and emergency management
source.implementation_notes: This must remain visible in copy/export.

redFlags.label: Severe DKA or high-risk age
redFlags.action: Escalate severe acidosis, bicarbonate below 5 mmol/L, pH below 7.1, age under 2 years, coma, seizure, or cardiovascular compromise to senior/PICU/retrieval pathway.
redFlags.rationale: RCH transfer guidance and NICE monitoring recommendations treat young age and severe DKA as high-risk for deterioration and cerebral edema.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Severe pediatric DKA, cerebral edema risk, shock, or need for high-acuity monitoring.
redFlags.management_change: Changes monitoring frequency, senior/PICU consultation, transfer, fluid/insulin supervision, and discharge exclusion.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; pH/bicarbonate and age thresholds define high-risk management.
redFlags.limitations: Use local PICU/retrieval thresholds when stricter than this general pathway.

source.implementation_notes: Do not bury severe-DKA thresholds in generic metadata.

redFlags.label: Shock, cardiovascular compromise, or repeated fluid bolus need
redFlags.action: Escalate shock, poor perfusion, hypotension, coma, or need for repeated boluses urgently; involve senior/PICU/retrieval when shock requires two 10 mL/kg boluses or local threshold is met.
redFlags.rationale: CHQ recommends urgent critical-care advice when shock requires repeated fluid boluses and RCH lists cardiovascular compromise as transfer-level risk.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Shock, severe dehydration, sepsis, HHS fluid deficit, or cardiovascular compromise.
redFlags.management_change: Changes bolus supervision, monitoring level, broad differential, sepsis source workup, ICU/retrieval route, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; shock/perfusion criteria drive emergency management.
redFlags.limitations: Treat shock immediately while avoiding unchecked adult large-volume fluid assumptions.

source.source_section: emergency management and shock fluid bolus
source.implementation_notes: Avoid adult-style fluid templates; use pediatric 10 mL/kg bolus logic and local escalation.

redFlags.label: Potassium danger or anuria
redFlags.action: Escalate hypokalemia, hyperkalemia, anuria, renal impairment, or ECG change before potassium-containing fluids or insulin changes.
redFlags.rationale: NICE and CHQ emphasize potassium rules: add potassium unless anuria or potassium is above normal, and address low potassium before insulin when present.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Hypokalemia, hyperkalemia, renal failure, arrhythmia risk, or unsafe insulin/potassium sequence.
redFlags.management_change: Changes potassium in fluids, insulin timing, ECG monitoring, senior consultation, renal/ICU involvement, and medication safety.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; potassium and urine output are direct treatment-safety thresholds.
redFlags.limitations: Do not add potassium blindly if anuric or potassium is above normal; do not start insulin before addressing severe hypokalemia per local protocol.

source.implementation_notes: Potassium exceptions must remain explicit in generated text.

redFlags.label: Pediatric HHS or mixed DKA/HHS concern
redFlags.action: Escalate very high glucose with osmolality above 320 mOsm/kg, severe dehydration, altered mental status, low ketones, or mixed DKA/HHS pattern for urgent endocrine/PICU advice.
redFlags.rationale: CHQ states HHS always requires urgent specialist advice and has different fluid and insulin timing than DKA.
redFlags.management_change: Changes osmolality monitoring, fluid replacement, insulin dose/timing, ICU involvement, and neurologic monitoring.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; HHS uses biochemical thresholds and high-acuity management rules.
redFlags.limitations: Do not start routine DKA insulin dosing when HHS glucose is falling with fluids and specialist advice is pending.

source.implementation_notes: HHS management should not be generated as generic DKA prose.

initialTests.label: Venous blood gas and acid-base status
initialTests.action: Obtain venous blood gas for pH, bicarbonate, and acid-base status at baseline and repeat per pediatric DKA protocol.
initialTests.rationale: DKA diagnosis and severity require acidosis assessment; repeated VBG helps track resolution and treatment safety.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: DKA severity, acidosis resolution, euglycemic DKA, mixed DKA/HHS, or alternative acid-base disorder.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order immediately for suspected pediatric DKA/HHS, ketones, vomiting with diabetes, altered status, Kussmaul breathing, or hyperglycemic crisis.
initialTests.management_change: pH below 7.3 or bicarbonate below 18 supports DKA; pH below 7.1 or bicarbonate below 5 changes severe-DKA escalation and monitoring.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: DKA: venous pH <7.3 and/or bicarbonate <18 mmol/L with ketones; severe risk: pH <7.1 or bicarbonate <5 mmol/L per pediatric escalation guidance.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; VBG values define diagnostic/severity thresholds.
initialTests.limitations: Interpret with ketones and glucose; euglycemic DKA can have lower glucose.

source.implementation_notes: Show thresholds in output, not just 'order labs'.

initialTests.label: Glucose and blood ketone thresholds
initialTests.action: Measure blood glucose and beta-hydroxybutyrate, or urine ketones if blood ketones unavailable; trend at protocol intervals.
initialTests.rationale: Glucose plus ketones distinguish DKA, euglycemic DKA, HHS, and treatment response.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: DKA, HHS, euglycemic DKA, mixed DKA/HHS, and resolution monitoring.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order immediately for suspected DKA/HHS and repeat hourly or per protocol during treatment.
initialTests.management_change: Glucose above 11 mmol/L with acidosis and moderate/large ketones supports DKA; glucose below 14 mmol/L during treatment triggers glucose-containing fluids; low ketones with glucose above 33.3 mmol/L raises HHS concern.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: DKA: BGL >11 mmol/L plus moderate/large ketones and acidosis; HHS: BGL >33.3 mmol/L with ketones <1.1 mmol/L and effective osmolality >320 mOsm/kg; add glucose to fluids when plasma glucose <14 mmol/L per NICE.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; glucose/ketones are direct diagnostic and treatment thresholds.
initialTests.limitations: Urine ketones can lag behind blood ketones and should not be the only treatment-response marker when blood ketones are available.

source.implementation_notes: Use mmol/L source thresholds; UI can support local unit conversion separately.

initialTests.label: Electrolytes, urea, creatinine, corrected sodium, and potassium
initialTests.action: Order U&E/renal function with potassium and sodium; calculate corrected sodium and repeat per protocol, including potassium check within 1 hour of insulin start.
initialTests.rationale: Potassium, sodium trajectory, renal function, and osmolality determine fluid composition, insulin safety, cerebral edema concern, and HHS routing.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Potassium danger, renal impairment, dehydration severity, corrected sodium trend, cerebral edema risk, and HHS osmolality.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order at baseline and repeat every 2 to 4 hours or per local pediatric DKA protocol; check potassium within 1 hour of starting insulin.
initialTests.management_change: Low potassium changes insulin timing/repletion; high potassium or anuria blocks potassium in fluids; falling sodium or rapid sodium change raises cerebral edema concern.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Add 40 mmol/L KCl to fluids unless anuria or potassium above normal; if hypokalemia at presentation, add potassium before insulin; track corrected sodium and osmolality.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; electrolyte results are direct treatment-safety thresholds.
initialTests.limitations: Use local pediatric reference ranges and pharmacy double-check for potassium-containing fluids.

source.implementation_notes: Potassium rules must be visible to prevent unsafe blanket recommendations.

initialTests.label: Effective osmolality for HHS or mixed DKA/HHS
initialTests.action: Calculate effective osmolality when glucose is very high, sodium is abnormal, mental status is abnormal, ketones are low, or HHS is possible.
initialTests.rationale: HHS diagnosis and neurologic risk depend on osmolality rather than glucose alone.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pediatric HHS, mixed DKA/HHS, hypernatremia/hyperosmolality, or neurologic risk.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order/calculate when BGL is very high, HHS features are present, ketones are low, or mental status is abnormal.
initialTests.management_change: Effective osmolality above 320 mOsm/kg changes specialist advice urgency, fluid replacement, insulin timing, and neurologic monitoring.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: HHS: effective osmolality >320 mOsm/kg with BGL >33.3 mmol/L, minimal ketones, and no major acidosis.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; osmolality is a diagnostic and treatment threshold.
initialTests.limitations: Use local formula/unit convention; mixed DKA/HHS may still have ketones and acidosis.

source.implementation_notes: Do not make embedding-only HHS suggestions authoritative without osmolality support.

initialTests.label: 12-lead ECG when potassium risk or severe illness
initialTests.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG when potassium is high, low, unknown with severe DKA, renal impairment exists, arrhythmia symptoms are present, or severe illness/shock is present.
initialTests.rationale: Potassium shifts in DKA can cause arrhythmias and change insulin/potassium management.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Hyperkalemia or hypokalemia ECG effects, arrhythmia, or severe electrolyte risk.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order when potassium abnormality/risk, renal impairment, ECG symptoms, shock, HHS, or severe DKA is present.
initialTests.management_change: ECG changes modify potassium replacement, insulin timing, cardiac monitoring, senior/PICU escalation, and transfer.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Use pediatric ECG interpretation; evaluate for potassium-related peaked T waves, widening QRS, flattened T/U waves, prolonged QT, or arrhythmia per local policy.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned; ECG identifies treatment-danger signs rather than DKA probability.
initialTests.limitations: Normal ECG does not rule out dangerous potassium trend; repeat labs per protocol.

source.source_section: face-to-face review and blood investigations including ECG
source.implementation_notes: ECG is targeted by potassium/severity risk, not a generic adult ACS row.

initialTests.label: New diabetes baseline labs and autoimmune/celiac screen
initialTests.action: For new diabetes, order HbA1c and local new-onset diabetes labs; consider thyroid function, IgA, and tissue transglutaminase antibodies per pediatric diabetes pathway.
initialTests.rationale: CHQ lists HbA1c and additional new diabetes tests including TFT/IgA/TTG; results support diagnosis, comorbidity screening, and follow-up.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: New pediatric diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease, celiac disease risk, and outpatient endocrine planning.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order when new diabetes, first DKA presentation, unclear type, or no prior pediatric diabetes workup is present.
initialTests.management_change: Results change diabetes classification, education, endocrine handoff, follow-up, and comorbidity screening; they do not delay acute DKA treatment.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Use local pediatric reference ranges and diabetes program requirements; do not delay fluids/insulin safety while waiting.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to the baseline new-diabetes lab bundle; acute management is based on DKA/HHS criteria.
initialTests.limitations: Do not use HbA1c alone to diagnose or exclude acute DKA.

source.source_section: baseline investigations and new diabetes tests
source.implementation_notes: Keep these separate from emergency DKA thresholds.

initialTests.label: Source-directed infection tests only when indicated
initialTests.action: Order urine testing/culture, blood cultures, CXR, viral testing, wound culture, or other infection tests only when fever, source symptoms, exam findings, sepsis concern, or senior advice supports them.
initialTests.rationale: CHQ notes high WCC is common in DKA and infection investigations should be guided by clinical features or senior advice.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: UTI, pneumonia, skin/line infection, sepsis, gastroenteritis, or other precipitating infection.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order when source-localizing symptoms/exam, persistent fever, sepsis physiology, immunocompromise, line/pump-site infection, or senior concern is present.
initialTests.management_change: Positive tests change antibiotics, source control, isolation, sepsis pathway, cultures follow-up, and discharge planning.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Do not use leukocytosis alone as infection proof in DKA; localize by symptoms, exam, cultures, imaging, and clinical trajectory.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to this test bundle; source-specific test interpretation depends on suspected infection.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay DKA stabilization for nonurgent source tests.

source.implementation_notes: Prevents generic fever modifier from creating broad unsupported recommendations.

dispositionRules.label: Emergency ABCD and senior escalation route
dispositionRules.action: Start with airway, breathing, circulation, disability assessment, IV access, bedside glucose/ketones, current weight, and senior pediatric/endocrine support; activate PICU/retrieval for unstable or high-risk children.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH and CHQ start pediatric DKA care with emergency ABCD assessment, IV access, weight, baseline bloods, hydration/neuro monitoring, and senior advice when high-risk features are present.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Unstable pediatric DKA/HHS, shock, altered conscious state, severe DKA, or treatment-safety risk.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes pacing from routine workup to emergency protocol, senior ownership, monitoring interval, transfer readiness, and handoff content.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to an emergency management rule.
dispositionRules.limitations: This module supports documentation and decision support; it does not replace local pediatric DKA/PICU protocol.

source.implementation_notes: Export should state escalation criteria plainly.

dispositionRules.label: Pediatric fluid and dextrose route
dispositionRules.action: Use pediatric protocol fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride initially, 10 mL/kg bolus for shock or poor perfusion per local pathway, strict fluid balance, and add glucose-containing fluids when plasma glucose falls below 14 mmol/L.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH/CHQ recommend 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus for shock/perfusion compromise, and NICE recommends 0.9% sodium chloride without glucose until plasma glucose falls below 14 mmol/L.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Dehydration, shock, glucose fall during DKA treatment, and cerebral edema risk.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes fluid choice, bolus size, dextrose addition, monitoring, oral/NPO plan, and repeated-bolus escalation.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to fluid management thresholds.
dispositionRules.limitations: HHS may need different fluid replacement and specialist advice; local protocol overrides this general rule.

source.implementation_notes: Avoid adult large-volume fluid assumptions.

dispositionRules.label: Insulin after fluids with no bolus
dispositionRules.action: Start IV insulin only after initial fluids have been running for about 1 hour, typically 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour per local protocol; never give an IV or IM insulin bolus for pediatric DKA/HHS.
dispositionRules.rationale: NICE and CHQ recommend starting IV insulin 1 to 2 hours after fluids and explicitly avoiding insulin bolus; CHQ lists 0.1 units/kg/hour with lower-dose options in selected children.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: DKA ketosis/acidosis treatment, hypoglycemia risk, potassium shift, and cerebral edema risk.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes timing, dose calculation, second-check requirement, pump discontinuation, potassium sequencing, and monitoring.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to insulin treatment timing.
dispositionRules.limitations: HHS insulin may be delayed and lower dose if glucose is falling with fluids; use urgent specialist advice.

source.implementation_notes: No-bolus language is safety critical.

dispositionRules.label: Potassium-safe fluid route
dispositionRules.action: Use potassium-containing fluids per pediatric protocol unless anuric or potassium is above normal; if potassium is low at presentation, address potassium before insulin infusion.
dispositionRules.rationale: NICE recommends 40 mmol/L potassium chloride in fluids except initial boluses unless anuria or potassium above normal, and adding potassium before insulin when hypokalemia is present.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Hypokalemia, hyperkalemia, arrhythmia risk, renal impairment, and insulin-induced potassium shift.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes fluid composition, insulin start, ECG/lab monitoring, pharmacy double-check, senior advice, and transfer threshold.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to potassium safety rules.
dispositionRules.limitations: Follow local electrolyte protocol and pharmacy/pediatric critical-care advice for severe hypo- or hyperkalemia.

source.source_section: potassium in fluids and insulin sequencing
source.implementation_notes: Potassium rule must remain separate from generic management prose.

dispositionRules.label: Avoid bicarbonate except specialist emergency indication
dispositionRules.action: Do not give bicarbonate routinely; consider only for life-threatening hyperkalemia or severe acidosis after discussion with pediatric intensivist/specialist per local protocol.
dispositionRules.rationale: NICE recommends no bicarbonate except life-threatening hyperkalemia or severe acidosis after pediatric intensivist discussion.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Severe acidosis, life-threatening hyperkalemia, and unsafe routine bicarbonate use.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes medication safety, specialist consultation, electrolyte monitoring, and emergency management.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to bicarbonate treatment limitation.
dispositionRules.limitations: This does not replace local PICU protocol for life-threatening hyperkalemia or profound acidosis.

source.implementation_notes: Show as a limitation/when-not-to-use rule to prevent overreach.

dispositionRules.label: Cerebral edema emergency treatment route
dispositionRules.action: If cerebral edema/oedema is suspected, do not wait for imaging; raise head of bed, give oxygen, urgently involve senior/PICU/retrieval, reassess fluids, and give hypertonic saline or mannitol per local pediatric protocol.
dispositionRules.rationale: CHQ directs immediate treatment for suspected cerebral edema/oedema and warns not to delay for neuroimaging.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Cerebral edema/oedema or raised intracranial pressure during pediatric DKA/HHS.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes emergency medication route, airway planning, fluid rate review, ICU/retrieval, monitoring, and transfer.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this emergency management rule.
dispositionRules.limitations: Intubation is high risk and should be specialist-led; local protocol and retrieval advice override generic output.

source.implementation_notes: This is a high-risk emergency pathway; keep wording concise and visible.

dispositionRules.label: Pediatric HHS specialist route
dispositionRules.action: For suspected HHS, seek urgent endocrine/PICU advice; prioritize fluid resuscitation and osmolality monitoring, and use insulin only when glucose is not falling with fluids alone at a lower dose per specialist protocol.
dispositionRules.rationale: CHQ states HHS always requires urgent specialist advice, can need more aggressive fluid replacement, and uses lower-dose insulin only after glucose is not falling with fluids.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes fluid replacement, insulin timing/dose, urinary loss replacement, neurologic/osmolality monitoring, ICU route, and transfer.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to HHS management routing.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not apply routine DKA insulin rate or timing to HHS without specialist advice.

source.implementation_notes: HHS must materially change output, not just add a chip.

dispositionRules.label: Diabetes team, education, and recurrence prevention route
dispositionRules.action: Before discharge/step-down, ensure pediatric diabetes-team handoff, sick-day and ketone plan, insulin/pump supply plan, caregiver/adolescent education, psychosocial/safeguarding review when indicated, and follow-up ownership.
dispositionRules.rationale: ISPAD emphasizes prevention of recurrent DKA through education, access support, and psychosocial assessment; NICE supports 24-hour diabetes-team support.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Unsafe discharge, recurrent DKA, insulin access barrier, device failure, psychosocial risk, or missed follow-up.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes discharge readiness, supplies, education, social work/psychology/safeguarding involvement, and follow-up interval.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to recurrence-prevention management.
dispositionRules.limitations: Keep copy/export PHI-safe; document sensitive details only in the clinical record per local policy.

source.implementation_notes: This route supports the improvement audit loop without exporting unnecessary PHI.
